Machine Learning in Materials Processing & Characterization
Unit 10: ML for Characterization Signals

Prof. Dr. Philipp Pelz

FAU Erlangen-Nürnberg

01. Learning Outcomes

After this unit, you will be able to:

  1. Describe the characteristics of 1D characterization signals (XRD, EELS, EDS, XPS, Raman) and why ML is needed
  1. Apply PCA to spectral data for dimensionality reduction and denoising
  1. Explain the architecture and training of autoencoders and their variants (DAE, CAE, VAE)
  1. Compare PCA and autoencoders for spectral compression and feature extraction
  1. Design a signal processing pipeline for hyperspectral materials data

Welcome to Unit 10. So far in this course we have focused on images and spatial data — micrographs, microstructure maps, time series from sensors. But a huge fraction of materials characterization produces one-dimensional signals: spectra. This unit is about applying the unsupervised learning methods we have studied — PCA and autoencoders — specifically to spectral data. We will see how compression, denoising, and phase identification can be automated using these tools, enabling the analysis of datasets with millions of spectra that would be impossible to handle manually.

02. Beyond Images: 1D Characterization Signals

  • Most of our course so far: spatial data (micrographs, microstructure maps)
  • But many instruments produce 1D spectral signals:
    • XRD — X-ray Diffraction: crystal structure via Bragg peaks
    • EELS — Electron Energy Loss Spectroscopy: bonding and electronic structure
    • EDS — Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy: elemental composition
    • XPS — X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy: surface chemistry and oxidation states
    • Raman — Vibrational spectroscopy: molecular fingerprints
  • Each technique produces a spectrum: intensity vs. energy (or angle, or wavenumber)
  • Modern instruments collect millions of spectra per experiment (spectrum imaging)

Let me introduce the zoo of spectral techniques that materials scientists use daily. X-ray diffraction gives us crystal structure through the positions and intensities of Bragg peaks. Electron energy loss spectroscopy reveals bonding, oxidation states, and electronic structure at atomic resolution. Energy dispersive spectroscopy maps elemental composition. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy probes surface chemistry. Raman spectroscopy provides molecular fingerprints through vibrational modes. What these all share is that they produce a one-dimensional signal — intensity as a function of some energy or angle variable. And modern instruments do not collect just one spectrum: spectrum imaging modes collect one spectrum per pixel, yielding datasets with millions of spectra.

03. The Nature of Characterization Signals

  • High dimensionality: A single spectrum may have 1024, 2048, or 4096 channels
    • Each spectrum is a point in \(\mathbb{R}^{2048}\)
  • Sparse peaks: Most channels contain background; only a few contain signal
  • Continuous backgrounds: Bremsstrahlung (EDS), plasmon tails (EELS), fluorescence
  • Noise: Shot noise (Poisson) dominates at low dose; detector noise adds Gaussian contributions
  • Variability: Peak positions shift with composition; peak shapes change with bonding

Spectra as Vectors

A spectrum with \(N\) channels is simply a vector \(\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^N\). All the linear algebra and ML tools we have learned apply directly — but the physical meaning of each “dimension” (an energy channel) matters for interpretation.

Let us think about what a spectrum looks like from the ML perspective. A 2048-channel EDS spectrum is a single point in a 2048-dimensional space. Most of these dimensions — most energy channels — contain only background and noise. The actual chemical information is concentrated in a handful of peaks. This is what we mean by sparsity: the information content is much lower than the dimensionality suggests. The noise follows Poisson statistics at low count rates, which means it scales with the square root of the signal — faint peaks are noisier relative to their height. Backgrounds from physical processes like bremsstrahlung or plasmon excitations create smooth, slowly varying contributions that must be separated from the peaks of interest. All of this makes spectra an ideal target for dimensionality reduction.

04. The Need for ML

Traditional Analysis Is Breaking Down

  • Manual peak fitting is slow, subjective, and does not scale
    • Fitting 10 peaks in 1 spectrum: feasible
    • Fitting 10 peaks in 1,000,000 spectra: impossible
  • Overlapping peaks make decomposition ambiguous
    • Fe-L and Mn-L edges in EELS overlap at ~640 eV
    • Ti-K\(\alpha\) and Ba-L\(\alpha\) overlap in EDS at ~4.5 keV
  • Subtle spectral changes encode critical physics
    • The Fe-L\(_{2,3}\) edge shape distinguishes Fe\(^{2+}\) from Fe\(^{3+}\)
    • Peak shift of 0.3 eV indicates a change in oxidation state
  • Batch effects: Instrument calibration drifts, detector aging, beam damage

Why do we need machine learning for spectra? Three reasons. First, scale: a modern scanning TEM with an EDS detector can collect a million spectra in a single map, each with 2048 channels. No human can fit peaks in all of those manually. Second, ambiguity: many elements have overlapping characteristic energies, and decomposing their contributions requires sophisticated methods. Third, subtlety: the difference between two oxidation states of iron is a slight change in the shape of the L-edge — something a trained spectroscopist might catch in one spectrum but cannot track across a million pixels. Machine learning excels at exactly these tasks: processing large volumes of data, decomposing overlapping signals, and detecting subtle systematic variations.

05. Goals: Compression, Denoising, Phase ID

Three Core Tasks for ML on Spectra

  1. Compression: Reduce \(\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{2048}\) to \(\mathbf{z} \in \mathbb{R}^{K}\) with \(K \ll 2048\)
    • Store and transmit massive spectral maps efficiently
  1. Denoising: Recover the clean signal from noisy measurements
    • Equivalent to faster acquisition or higher beam current — without the dose
  1. Phase Identification: Group similar spectra and identify distinct phases
    • Automated chemical/structural mapping across entire specimens
  • All three tasks are connected through dimensionality reduction
  • If we find the right low-dimensional representation, compression, denoising, and clustering all follow naturally

These three tasks — compression, denoising, and phase identification — are the workhorses of spectral data analysis. And they are deeply connected. If we can find a good low-dimensional representation of our spectra, then compression is automatic because we only store the low-dimensional codes. Denoising follows because noise lives in the high-dimensional complement that we discard. And phase identification becomes a clustering problem in the low-dimensional space, which is much easier than clustering in 2048 dimensions. The tools we will study — PCA and autoencoders — are both methods for learning this low-dimensional representation. PCA finds the best linear representation; autoencoders find non-linear ones.

Section 2: PCA for Spectra

Eigenspectra, Scree Plots, and Linear Denoising

In this section we apply Principal Component Analysis specifically to spectral data. PCA is often the first tool a materials scientist should reach for when faced with a large spectral dataset. It is fast, mathematically well-understood, and provides a unique solution. We will see how to interpret the principal components as “eigenspectra,” how to choose the number of components using scree plots, and how to denoise spectra by reconstructing from a truncated basis.

07. PCA for Spectra

The Classical Tool for Spectral Decomposition (Sandfeld et al. 2024)

  • Given \(N\) spectra, each with \(D\) channels, form the data matrix \(\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times D}\)
  • PCA finds orthogonal directions of maximum variance in this \(D\)-dimensional space
  • Each spectrum can be approximated as a linear combination of \(K\) basis vectors:

\[\mathbf{x}_i \approx \bar{\mathbf{x}} + \sum_{k=1}^{K} c_{ik} \, \mathbf{v}_k\]

  • \(\bar{\mathbf{x}}\): mean spectrum (average over all \(N\) spectra)
  • \(\mathbf{v}_k\): the \(k\)-th eigenvector of the covariance matrix (the \(k\)-th eigenspectrum)
  • \(c_{ik}\): the score (coefficient) of spectrum \(i\) on component \(k\)
  • Compression: Store only the \(K\) scores \(c_{ik}\) instead of the full \(D\) channels

PCA for spectra works as follows. We collect all our spectra into a matrix — each row is one spectrum, each column is one energy channel. PCA computes the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix of this data. These eigenvectors are themselves spectra — they have the same shape and the same number of channels — and we call them eigenspectra. Any spectrum in our dataset can be approximated as the mean spectrum plus a weighted sum of eigenspectra. The weights, or scores, tell us “how much” of each eigenspectrum is present. This is covered in Sandfeld Chapter 15.2. The key insight is that if the data has low intrinsic dimensionality — if a few phases or chemical variations explain most of the spectral variation — then we need only a few eigenspectra to represent the data well.

08. The Basis Function View: Eigenspectra

flowchart LR
    A["Raw Spectrum<br>x ∈ ℝ²⁰⁴⁸"] --> B["Center<br>x - x̄"]
    B --> C["Project onto<br>Eigenspectra V"]
    C --> D["Score Vector<br>c ∈ ℝᴷ"]
    D --> E["Reconstruct<br>x̂ = x̄ + Vc"]
    E --> F["Denoised<br>Spectrum"]

    style A fill:#2d5016,stroke:#4a8c2a,color:#fff
    style D fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#2a6a9c,color:#fff
    style F fill:#5c1a1a,stroke:#9c2a2a,color:#fff

  • The eigenspectra \(\{\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{v}_K\}\) form an orthonormal basis
  • Each eigenspectrum is a “building block” — a spectral shape that varies across the dataset
  • The scores \(c_{ik} = \mathbf{v}_k^\top (\mathbf{x}_i - \bar{\mathbf{x}})\) are computed by simple inner products
  • This is computationally cheap: once the eigenspectra are computed, projecting a new spectrum is a matrix-vector multiply

Think of the eigenspectra as building blocks or basis functions. Every spectrum in the dataset can be assembled from these blocks. The first eigenspectrum captures the most common pattern of variation. The second captures the next most common pattern, orthogonal to the first, and so on. The scores tell you the recipe: how much of each building block to use for a given spectrum. Computing the scores for a new spectrum is trivially fast — it is just a dot product with each eigenspectrum. This makes PCA ideal for real-time applications where you need to process spectra as they arrive from the instrument.

09. Interpreting Eigenspectra

  • PC1 (first eigenspectrum): Typically resembles the mean spectrum
    • Captures the dominant overall shape (background + major peaks)
  • PC2, PC3: Capture the dominant variations
    • Often correspond to specific chemical differences between phases
    • Example: PC2 might show positive Fe peaks and negative Cr peaks (Fe vs. Cr variation)
  • Higher PCs (\(k > K_{\text{signal}}\)): Capture noise
    • Appear as random oscillations with no physical meaning

Interpreting the eigenspectra is crucial. The first component is almost always close to the mean spectrum because the largest source of variance is the overall intensity. The second and third components reveal the most important chemical or structural contrasts in the dataset. For instance, in an EDS map of a steel sample, PC2 might show positive peaks at the iron K-alpha energy and negative peaks at the chromium K-alpha energy — meaning that PC2 captures the variation between iron-rich and chromium-rich regions. Higher components eventually become noise — they look like random wiggles with no discernible peak structure. The transition from signal to noise components is what the scree plot will help us identify.

10. Intrinsic Dimensionality

  • A 2048-channel spectrum lives in \(\mathbb{R}^{2048}\) — but how many dimensions does the data actually use?
  • If the sample has 4 distinct phases, the spectra approximately span a 4-dimensional subspace
  • The intrinsic dimensionality \(K\) is the number of independent spectral variations
  • For most materials datasets: \(K \sim 3\text{--}20 \ll D = 2048\)

Rule of Thumb

The intrinsic dimensionality of a spectral dataset is typically close to the number of distinct phases or chemical environments in the sample, plus a few components for background variations and thickness effects.

This is the key insight that makes PCA so powerful for spectral data. Even though each spectrum has 2048 channels, the data does not fill all 2048 dimensions. Instead, it lies close to a low-dimensional subspace whose dimension equals the number of independent spectral variations. In a sample with four phases, you might need four to six components: one for each phase, plus a couple for background and thickness variations. Everything beyond that is noise. This massive gap between the ambient dimensionality (2048) and the intrinsic dimensionality (say, 6) is what enables both compression and denoising.

11. Scree Plots: Choosing the Number of Components

How Many PCs to Keep? (Sandfeld et al. 2024)

  • The scree plot shows the explained variance (eigenvalue) for each component
  • Signal components have large eigenvalues; noise components have small, roughly equal eigenvalues

  • The Elbow Rule: Keep components before the “elbow” where eigenvalues level off
  • Cumulative variance: Often 95% or 99% of total variance is captured by \(K \sim 5\text{--}15\) components
  • Alternative: Parallel analysis — compare eigenvalues against those from random data

The scree plot is the standard tool for deciding how many components to keep. You plot the eigenvalue — which is the variance explained by that component — against the component index. The signal components have large eigenvalues that decrease rapidly. The noise components have small, roughly constant eigenvalues that form a “scree” (like the rubble at the base of a cliff). The elbow, where the curve transitions from steep to flat, tells you where signal ends and noise begins. In practice, for most materials spectral datasets, you find that 5 to 15 components capture 95 to 99 percent of the total variance. This is discussed in Sandfeld Section 15.3.2.

12. Denoising via Reconstruction

Truncated PCA as a Filter (Sandfeld et al. 2024)

Algorithm:

  1. Compute eigenspectra from the dataset: \(\mathbf{V}_K = [\mathbf{v}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{v}_K]\)
  2. Project each noisy spectrum: \(\mathbf{c}_i = \mathbf{V}_K^\top (\mathbf{x}_i - \bar{\mathbf{x}})\)
  3. Reconstruct: \(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_i = \bar{\mathbf{x}} + \mathbf{V}_K \mathbf{c}_i\)
  • The reconstruction \(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_i\) retains only the signal subspace — noise is discarded

  • Equivalent to reducing acquisition time by 10x while maintaining chemical sensitivity

PCA denoising is remarkably simple and effective. You compute the eigenspectra, project each noisy spectrum onto the first K components, and reconstruct. The reconstruction contains only the signal — the noise, which lives in the discarded components, is gone. This is equivalent to applying a filter that is optimally adapted to your data. In practical terms, PCA denoising of EDS spectrum images can give you the same signal-to-noise ratio as a 10x longer acquisition, but without the additional electron dose that can damage beam-sensitive samples. This is covered in Sandfeld Section 15.3.3 and is one of the most impactful applications of PCA in electron microscopy.

13. Think About This…

PCA Denoising: Free Lunch?

Question: If PCA denoising is so effective, why not always reduce acquisition time by 10x and denoise afterwards?

Hint: Think about what happens when a spectrum contains a feature that is unique — present in only one or two pixels of the map.

Consider:

  • PCA eigenspectra are computed from the entire dataset
  • Rare features may not contribute enough variance to be captured by the top-\(K\) components
  • PCA denoising can erase rare phases — minority signals get projected away
  • This is a fundamental tension: denoising vs. discovery

Key Insight

PCA denoising is excellent for known, common features but can suppress rare or unexpected signals. Always inspect the residuals \(\mathbf{x}_i - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_i\) for signs of discarded information.

This is an important point that is often overlooked. PCA denoising works by projecting onto the subspace of maximum variance. But rare phases — a small precipitate, a trace contaminant, a single grain with an unusual orientation — contribute very little variance to the dataset. Their spectral signatures may end up in the noise components and get discarded during denoising. This means PCA denoising can actually erase the most scientifically interesting features in your data. The solution is to always check the residuals — the difference between the original and reconstructed spectra — and to consider using more components than the scree plot suggests if you are in discovery mode. This tension between denoising and discovery is a recurring theme in data-driven materials science.

14. PCA Limitations

The Linearity Constraint

  • PCA assumes that spectral variations can be expressed as linear combinations
  • This works well when: phases mix additively (Beer-Lambert law, EDS from thin sections)
  • PCA fails when:
    • Peak positions shift with composition (e.g., XRD peak shift with lattice parameter)
    • Peak shapes change non-linearly (e.g., EELS fine structure with oxidation state)
    • Backgrounds are multiplicative rather than additive
  • PCs are orthogonal — but physical phases are not orthogonal
    • PCs can have negative values, but spectra are non-negative
    • Alternative: NMF (Non-negative Matrix Factorization) enforces \(\mathbf{V} \geq 0\), \(\mathbf{C} \geq 0\)
  • These limitations motivate autoencoders: non-linear generalizations of PCA

PCA has fundamental limitations that stem from its linearity. If the spectral variations in your dataset are not well described by linear combinations, PCA will struggle. The most common failure mode in materials science is peak shifts: when a diffraction peak moves to a different angle because the lattice parameter has changed, PCA cannot represent this with a single component. It needs multiple components to approximate the shift, which wastes dimensionality on a single physical effect. Another issue is that PCA eigenvectors are orthogonal and can have negative values, which makes physical interpretation difficult — a spectrum cannot have negative intensity. NMF, Non-negative Matrix Factorization, addresses this by constraining both the basis spectra and the coefficients to be non-negative. But for truly non-linear variations, we need autoencoders.

15. Summary: PCA for Spectra

Aspect PCA
Type Linear dimensionality reduction
Speed Very fast (SVD or eigendecomposition)
Solution Unique, deterministic
Interpretation Eigenspectra = orthogonal basis functions
Denoising Truncated reconstruction
Compression Store \(K\) scores instead of \(D\) channels
Limitation Cannot handle non-linear variations

When to use PCA:

  • As the first step in any spectral analysis pipeline
  • When variations are approximately linear (additive mixing)
  • When you need fast, reproducible results
  • For denoising large spectral maps

Let me summarize PCA for spectra. It is fast, deterministic, well-understood, and gives you a unique solution. It is the right first step for almost any spectral dataset. Use it for denoising, compression, and as a preprocessing step before clustering or classification. But remember its limitations: it assumes linearity, its components are orthogonal (which physical phases are not), and it can suppress rare features. When these limitations matter, autoencoders offer a powerful alternative, which we will explore next.

Section 3: Autoencoders for Signals

Non-linear Compression, Denoising, and Feature Extraction

Now we move to autoencoders — the deep learning generalization of PCA. Autoencoders can learn non-linear representations and handle the complex spectral variations that PCA struggles with. We will cover the basic architecture, training, variants like denoising and variational autoencoders, and how they compare to PCA for spectral analysis tasks.

17. Autoencoder Concept

A Neural Network That Learns to Compress (Sandfeld et al. 2024)

  • An autoencoder (AE) is a neural network trained to reconstruct its own input
  • The identity function \(f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}\) is trivial — so we add a bottleneck
  • The network must learn a compressed representation \(\mathbf{z}\) that captures the essential information

\[\mathbf{z} = f_{\text{enc}}(\mathbf{x}; \theta_{\text{enc}}) \quad \quad \hat{\mathbf{x}} = f_{\text{dec}}(\mathbf{z}; \theta_{\text{dec}})\]

  • If \(\dim(\mathbf{z}) \ll \dim(\mathbf{x})\), the AE must discover meaningful features
  • With linear activations and one hidden layer, an AE recovers PCA (Sandfeld et al. 2024)
  • With non-linear activations, it learns more powerful representations

The autoencoder concept is beautifully simple. You have a neural network whose input and output are the same: a spectrum. The network has a narrow bottleneck in the middle — the latent space — that forces it to compress the input. If the bottleneck has, say, 10 dimensions, the network must figure out how to squeeze a 2048-dimensional spectrum into 10 numbers and then reconstruct the full spectrum from those 10 numbers. The remarkable fact is that with linear activations, this is exactly PCA. But with non-linear activations like ReLU, the autoencoder can learn curved manifolds and capture non-linear relationships that PCA misses. This is discussed in Sandfeld Chapter 19.4.

18. AE Architecture: The Hourglass

flowchart LR
    subgraph Encoder
        I["Input<br>2048 channels"] --> H1["Dense 512<br>ReLU"]
        H1 --> H2["Dense 128<br>ReLU"]
        H2 --> H3["Dense 32<br>ReLU"]
    end

    subgraph Bottleneck
        H3 --> Z["Latent z<br>dim = 8"]
    end

    subgraph Decoder
        Z --> H4["Dense 32<br>ReLU"]
        H4 --> H5["Dense 128<br>ReLU"]
        H5 --> H6["Dense 512<br>ReLU"]
        H6 --> O["Output<br>2048 channels"]
    end

    style I fill:#2d5016,stroke:#4a8c2a,color:#fff
    style Z fill:#8B0000,stroke:#cc3333,color:#fff
    style O fill:#2d5016,stroke:#4a8c2a,color:#fff

  • Encoder \(f_{\text{enc}}\): Progressively reduces dimensionality (2048 → 512 → 128 → 32 → 8)
  • Bottleneck \(\mathbf{z}\): The latent representation — the “compressed DNA” of the spectrum
  • Decoder \(f_{\text{dec}}\): Mirrors the encoder, reconstructing from the latent code (8 → 32 → 128 → 512 → 2048)

The hourglass architecture is the defining feature of an autoencoder. The encoder progressively squeezes the 2048-channel input through layers with decreasing width, like pressing information through a funnel. At the narrowest point — the bottleneck — the entire spectrum is represented by just 8 numbers. Then the decoder expands it back out, reconstructing the original 2048 channels. The architecture is symmetric: the decoder mirrors the encoder. Each hidden layer uses ReLU activations to introduce non-linearity. The compression ratio here is 2048:8, or 256:1. The fact that the network can reconstruct spectra from just 8 numbers tells us something profound about the intrinsic dimensionality of the data.

19. Why the Bottleneck?

  • Without a bottleneck, the network would learn the identity function — trivial and useless
  • The bottleneck creates an information bottleneck: the network must decide what to keep and what to discard
  • What gets kept: systematic, recurring patterns — peaks, backgrounds, spectral shapes shared across the dataset
  • What gets discarded: random, non-repeating variations — noise, detector artifacts, cosmic rays
  • The bottleneck size is a hyperparameter:
    • Too large: noise passes through (undercomplete but still noisy)
    • Too small: signal is lost (over-compressed)
    • Just right: captures the intrinsic dimensionality

The bottleneck is the entire point of the autoencoder. Without it, a sufficiently large network would simply learn to copy its input, including all the noise. The bottleneck forces the network to prioritize: with only 8 latent dimensions, it must focus on the features that appear consistently across the training set and allow it to reconstruct the most spectra most accurately. Noise, by definition, is different in every spectrum — so the network cannot afford to waste limited bottleneck capacity representing it. The bottleneck size is the main hyperparameter: too large and noise leaks through, too small and you lose signal. In practice, you tune this by monitoring the reconstruction error on a validation set, similar to choosing the number of PCA components.

20. Non-linearity: The Power of AE vs PCA

  • PCA: Finds the best linear subspace (a hyperplane)
  • AE: Finds the best non-linear manifold (a curved surface)
  • Example — XRD peak shift:
    • As lattice parameter \(a\) increases, the (111) peak shifts to lower \(2\theta\)
    • PCA needs multiple components to approximate this shift
    • An AE learns: “one latent variable controls peak position” — direct physical meaning

  • Non-linearity lets the AE learn physically meaningful latent variables

This is the key advantage of autoencoders over PCA. Consider an XRD dataset where the only variation is a shift in peak position due to a changing lattice parameter. PCA must approximate this continuous shift using linear combinations of fixed basis vectors — which requires multiple components to achieve a decent approximation. An autoencoder, with its non-linear activation functions, can learn a single latent variable that smoothly controls the peak position. This is a much more natural and compact representation. The same argument applies to peak shape changes in EELS, peak broadening due to strain, and non-linear background variations. Whenever the spectral variations are inherently non-linear, autoencoders will outperform PCA.

21. Training: Reconstruction Loss

  • The training objective is to minimize the reconstruction error:

\[\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \|\mathbf{x}_i - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_i\|^2\]

  • This is the mean squared error (MSE) between input and output spectra
  • Training is unsupervised: no labels needed — the spectrum itself is the target
  • Optimization: Adam optimizer, mini-batch gradient descent
  • Training on \(10^5\)–\(10^6\) spectra typically takes minutes on a GPU
  • Alternative losses:
    • Poisson negative log-likelihood: Better for photon-counting data
    • Spectral angle distance: Focuses on spectral shape, not intensity

Training the autoencoder is straightforward because it is unsupervised — we do not need any labels. The target for each spectrum is the spectrum itself. The standard loss function is the mean squared error between the input spectrum and the reconstructed spectrum, summed over all channels and averaged over all training spectra. We optimize using Adam and mini-batches. For spectral data, we might want to use alternative loss functions: the Poisson negative log-likelihood is more appropriate for photon-counting data where the noise variance depends on the signal level. The spectral angle distance focuses on the shape of the spectrum rather than its absolute intensity, which can be useful when thickness or dose variations are not of interest.

22. Latent Space Properties

  • The latent variables \(\mathbf{z}\) often develop physically interpretable meanings during training
  • Examples of latent-physical correlations:
    • \(z_1 \leftrightarrow\) specimen thickness (controls overall intensity)
    • \(z_2 \leftrightarrow\) Fe/Cr ratio (controls relative peak heights)
    • \(z_3 \leftrightarrow\) oxidation state (controls edge fine structure)
  • These correlations are emergent — not programmed, but discovered by the network
  • The latent space can be used for:
    • Clustering: Group spectra by latent coordinates
    • Visualization: Plot \(z_1\) vs. \(z_2\) as a chemical map
    • Interpolation: Generate intermediate spectra by moving through latent space

One of the most fascinating aspects of autoencoders is that the latent variables often develop physically meaningful interpretations. The network is not told about thickness or composition — it discovers these concepts because they are the most efficient way to explain the variations in the data. You can verify this by plotting each latent variable against known physical quantities and looking for correlations. When you find them, the autoencoder has effectively learned a physics-aware representation of your spectral data. This latent space becomes a powerful tool for downstream analysis: you can cluster spectra in latent space, visualize the latent coordinates as spatial maps, or interpolate between spectra by moving smoothly through the latent space.

23. Denoising Autoencoders (DAE)

Learning the Clean Manifold (McClarren 2021)

  • Standard AE: Input = clean spectrum, Target = clean spectrum
  • Denoising AE: Input = noisy spectrum, Target = clean spectrum

\[\mathcal{L}_{\text{DAE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \|\mathbf{x}_i^{\text{clean}} - f_{\text{dec}}(f_{\text{enc}}(\mathbf{x}_i^{\text{clean}} + \boldsymbol{\eta}_i))\|^2\]

  • The noise \(\boldsymbol{\eta}_i\) can be:
    • Gaussian: \(\eta \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)\)
    • Poisson: Resample from \(\text{Poisson}(\mathbf{x}_i^{\text{clean}})\)
    • Masking: Randomly zero out spectral channels
  • The DAE learns to project noisy spectra back onto the clean data manifold

DAE vs. PCA Denoising

Both discard noise by projecting onto a low-dimensional representation. But a DAE explicitly trains on noisy-clean pairs, making it more robust when the noise model is known. PCA denoising is implicit — it assumes noise is in the discarded components.

Denoising autoencoders take the denoising idea a step further than PCA. Instead of implicitly discarding noise by truncating the basis, a DAE is explicitly trained to map noisy inputs to clean outputs. During training, we corrupt each clean spectrum with realistic noise — Poisson noise for photon counting, Gaussian noise for detector readout — and train the network to recover the original clean spectrum. The DAE learns a mapping from the “noisy cloud” around the data manifold back onto the manifold itself. This is discussed in McClarren Section 8.3.2. The advantage over PCA denoising is that the DAE can be tailored to a specific noise model and can handle non-linear features that PCA would smear.

24. DAE Example: EELS Spectra

Denoising the Fe-L\(_{2,3}\) Edge

  • Problem: At low dose, the EELS fine structure that distinguishes Fe\(^{2+}\) from Fe\(^{3+}\) is buried in noise
  • Approach:
    1. Train a DAE on simulated Fe-L edge spectra with known oxidation states
    2. Add Poisson noise at realistic dose levels during training
    3. Apply trained DAE to experimental spectra pixel-by-pixel

  • Result: Clear Fe\(^{2+}\)/Fe\(^{3+}\) discrimination at 10x lower dose than conventional analysis

Let me give you a concrete example. The iron L-edge in EELS has a characteristic fine structure that depends on the oxidation state: the ratio of the L3 to L2 peak, and the shape of the L3 white line, are different for Fe-two-plus and Fe-three-plus. At high doses, a trained spectroscopist can distinguish these by eye. But at low doses — which are necessary for beam-sensitive samples — the fine structure is buried in shot noise. A denoising autoencoder trained on simulated spectra with known oxidation states can recover the fine structure from noisy experimental data. The result is an oxidation state map with spatial resolution and chemical sensitivity that would otherwise require doses high enough to damage the sample. This is a real and impactful application of DAEs in electron microscopy.

25. Convolutional Autoencoders for Spectra

  • Standard AEs use fully connected (dense) layers — every channel connects to every neuron
  • Problem: Dense layers do not know that nearby channels are related
  • 1D Convolutional AE: Replace dense layers with 1D convolution + pooling
  • Advantages:
    • Shift invariance: A peak at 640 eV is recognized the same way as at 650 eV
    • Fewer parameters: Weight sharing across the spectral axis
    • Local feature detection: Learns peak shapes, edge onsets, and local patterns
  • Architecture: Input → [Conv1D → ReLU → MaxPool1D] × 3 → Latent → [ConvTranspose1D → ReLU] × 3 → Output
  • Particularly effective for XRD and Raman where peak shapes are well-defined

Convolutional autoencoders bring the same advantages to spectral data that CNNs bring to images. Instead of dense layers that treat each channel independently, 1D convolutional layers learn local patterns — peak shapes, edge onsets, background curvature — that can appear anywhere along the spectral axis. This shift invariance is critical for spectra: a peak is a peak regardless of its energy position. The weight sharing in convolutions also dramatically reduces the number of parameters, which helps when training data is limited. The encoder uses 1D convolutions with max pooling to progressively compress the spectrum, and the decoder uses transposed convolutions to expand it back. This architecture is particularly effective for XRD patterns, where the signal consists of well-defined peaks at various positions.

26. Variational Autoencoders (VAE)

  • Standard AE: The latent space \(\mathbf{z}\) has no structure — just whatever the network finds useful
  • VAE: The encoder outputs a probability distribution \(q(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x}) = \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\sigma}^2)\)
  • Training loss adds a KL divergence term:

\[\mathcal{L}_{\text{VAE}} = \underbrace{\|\mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}\|^2}_{\text{reconstruction}} + \beta \cdot \underbrace{D_{\text{KL}}(q(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x}) \| p(\mathbf{z}))}_{\text{regularization}}\]

  • The KL term encourages the latent space to be smooth and continuous
    • Nearby points in latent space produce similar spectra
    • You can sample from the latent space to generate new, realistic spectra
  • Materials application: Generate synthetic training spectra, explore phase space, uncertainty estimation

Variational autoencoders add probabilistic structure to the latent space. Instead of mapping each spectrum to a single point in latent space, the VAE maps it to a probability distribution — parameterized by a mean and variance. The KL divergence term in the loss function regularizes the latent space to be smooth and continuous, meaning that interpolating between two points in latent space produces valid intermediate spectra. This is useful in materials science for several reasons: you can generate synthetic spectra to augment training data, you can explore what spectra would look like at compositions you have not measured, and the variance of the latent distribution gives you a measure of uncertainty. The beta parameter controls the trade-off between reconstruction quality and latent space smoothness.

27. PCA vs. Autoencoder: Head-to-Head

Feature PCA Autoencoder
Linearity Linear only Non-linear
Speed Very fast (SVD) Slower (GPU training)
Solution Unique, deterministic Depends on initialization
Interpretability Eigenspectra are intuitive Latent space needs probing
Data requirement Works with small datasets Needs more data
Peak shifts Needs many components Handles naturally
Denoising Truncated reconstruction DAE with noise model
Generative No Yes (VAE)

Practical recommendation:

  • Start with PCA — it is fast, unique, and gives you a baseline
  • Switch to AE when PCA reconstruction error plateaus or non-linear effects are important
  • Use DAE when you have a good noise model
  • Use VAE when you need generative capabilities or uncertainty

Let me give you a practical comparison. PCA should always be your first step. It is fast, deterministic, and gives you a unique solution that serves as a baseline. If PCA reconstruction error is already low with a few components, you may not need an autoencoder at all. Switch to an autoencoder when you see that PCA needs many components to achieve acceptable reconstruction — this suggests non-linear variations that an AE can capture more efficiently. Use a denoising autoencoder when you know the noise model, especially for low-dose electron microscopy. Use a variational autoencoder when you want to generate synthetic spectra or need uncertainty estimates. In practice, many papers report both PCA and AE results to demonstrate that the AE provides a meaningful improvement.

28. Think About This…

The Latent Space as a Chemical Compass

Question: You train an autoencoder with a 2D latent space on EDS spectra from a ternary alloy (Fe-Cr-Ni). You plot the latent coordinates for each pixel and color them by known composition. What do you expect to see?

Hint: The alloy has three independent compositional degrees of freedom, but they sum to 100%.

Consider:

  • Three elements with the constraint \(c_{\text{Fe}} + c_{\text{Cr}} + c_{\text{Ni}} = 1\) means two independent compositional variables
  • A 2D latent space should be sufficient to capture this variation
  • You would expect the latent space to look like a triangle (the ternary phase diagram!)
  • The autoencoder has rediscovered the Gibbs triangle — without being told anything about thermodynamics
  • Follow-up: What if you used a 1D latent space? What would be lost?

This is a wonderful example of how autoencoders can rediscover physics. A ternary alloy has two independent compositional degrees of freedom because the three concentrations must sum to one. If you train an autoencoder with a 2D latent space on EDS spectra from such an alloy, the latent space will spontaneously organize itself into a shape resembling the ternary phase diagram — a triangle with the pure elements at the corners. The network was never told about thermodynamics or phase diagrams; it discovered this structure because it is the most efficient way to represent the data. If you used a 1D latent space, you would lose information — the network would have to project the 2D compositional variation onto a line, losing the ability to distinguish compositions that differ in the orthogonal direction.

29. Anomaly Detection with Autoencoders

  • Train the AE on “normal” spectra from the expected phases
  • For a new spectrum \(\mathbf{x}\), compute the reconstruction error: \(e = \|\mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}\|^2\)
  • If \(e > \tau\) (threshold), the spectrum is anomalous — it does not fit the learned representation
  • Materials applications:
    • Detecting unexpected phases or contamination
    • Flagging instrument malfunctions (detector artifacts, calibration drift)
    • Finding rare events (nano-precipitates, grain boundary segregation)

  • Unlike supervised anomaly detection, this requires no labels — only knowledge of what “normal” looks like

Anomaly detection is one of the most powerful and underused applications of autoencoders in materials science. The idea is simple: if an autoencoder has learned to reconstruct the normal spectra in your dataset well, then any spectrum that is significantly different from normal will have a high reconstruction error. By mapping the reconstruction error spatially, you create an anomaly map that highlights unusual regions — unexpected phases, contamination, grain boundary segregation, or even instrument artifacts. This is entirely unsupervised: you do not need to know in advance what the anomalies are, only what “normal” looks like. The threshold tau can be set using a percentile of the training reconstruction errors, or using a statistical test.

30. Summary: Autoencoders for Spectra

  • Autoencoders are the non-linear generalization of PCA for spectral data
  • Key variants:
    • Standard AE: Compression and feature extraction
    • DAE: Denoising with explicit noise models
    • CAE: 1D convolutions for shift-invariant peak detection
    • VAE: Probabilistic latent space for generation and uncertainty
  • The latent space is the central concept — it provides:
    • A compressed representation for storage
    • A denoised representation for analysis
    • A feature space for clustering and classification
    • A basis for anomaly detection
  • Best practice: Always compare AE results against PCA as a baseline

Let me summarize autoencoders for spectra. They extend PCA into the non-linear domain, handling peak shifts, shape changes, and complex backgrounds that PCA struggles with. The four main variants — standard AE, denoising AE, convolutional AE, and variational AE — address different aspects of the spectral analysis problem. The latent space is the unifying concept: it provides compression, denoising, features for clustering, and a basis for anomaly detection, all in one representation. Always compare against PCA to verify that the additional complexity of the autoencoder is justified by improved performance.

Section 4: Practical Signal Processing

Pipelines, Hyperspectral Data, and Transfer Learning

In this section we turn to the practical aspects of building signal processing pipelines for materials characterization data. We will cover normalization, the challenge of hyperspectral data cubes, spatial-spectral autoencoders, signal unmixing, and transfer learning from simulated to experimental data. These are the engineering considerations that determine whether your ML model works in the lab.

32. Signal Processing Workflow

flowchart TD
    A["Raw Spectra<br>(N × D)"] --> B["Preprocessing"]
    B --> B1["Background Subtraction"]
    B --> B2["Normalization"]
    B --> B3["Alignment / Calibration"]
    B1 & B2 & B3 --> C["Dimensionality Reduction"]
    C --> C1["PCA"]
    C --> C2["Autoencoder"]
    C1 & C2 --> D["Latent Representation<br>(N × K)"]
    D --> E1["Clustering<br>(Phase Maps)"]
    D --> E2["Anomaly Detection<br>(Rare Phases)"]
    D --> E3["Classification<br>(Known Phases)"]

    style A fill:#2d5016,stroke:#4a8c2a,color:#fff
    style D fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#2a6a9c,color:#fff
    style E1 fill:#5c1a1a,stroke:#9c2a2a,color:#fff
    style E2 fill:#5c1a1a,stroke:#9c2a2a,color:#fff
    style E3 fill:#5c1a1a,stroke:#9c2a2a,color:#fff

  • Preprocessing: Prepare spectra for ML (remove artifacts, normalize, align)
  • Dimensionality Reduction: PCA or AE to extract the latent representation
  • Downstream Analysis: Clustering, anomaly detection, or classification in latent space

This is the standard workflow for ML-based spectral analysis. It has three stages. First, preprocessing: you need to subtract backgrounds, normalize the spectra, and align the energy axis to account for calibration drift. These steps are critical — garbage in, garbage out. Second, dimensionality reduction: PCA or an autoencoder maps each high-dimensional spectrum to a compact latent representation. Third, downstream analysis: you use the latent representation for whatever your scientific question requires — clustering to identify phases, anomaly detection to find unexpected features, or classification against a library of known phases. Each of these stages has important practical considerations that we will now discuss.

33. Normalization Strategies

Why Normalize?

  • Raw spectral intensities depend on dose, dwell time, specimen thickness — not just chemistry
  • Without normalization, PCA/AE will learn intensity variations, not chemical variations

Common Strategies

  1. Total-count normalization: \(\mathbf{x}' = \mathbf{x} / \sum_j x_j\)
    • Makes all spectra have unit area — removes thickness/dose effects
  1. Peak-height normalization: \(\mathbf{x}' = \mathbf{x} / \max_j x_j\)
    • Makes the strongest peak equal to 1 — preserves relative peak ratios
  1. Standard Normal Variate (SNV): \(\mathbf{x}' = (\mathbf{x} - \bar{x}) / s_x\)
    • Centers and scales each spectrum individually — common in chemometrics
  1. Min-max per channel: Normalize each energy channel across the dataset
    • Ensures all channels contribute equally to the loss

Normalization is the most important preprocessing step and the one most often done wrong. The fundamental issue is that raw spectral intensities depend on many factors beyond chemistry: specimen thickness, beam current, dwell time, detector efficiency. If you feed raw spectra to PCA or an autoencoder, the first component will capture intensity variations — which are usually not what you care about. Total-count normalization removes this by dividing each spectrum by its integrated area. Peak-height normalization divides by the maximum intensity. SNV centers and scales each spectrum to zero mean and unit variance. The right choice depends on your data and your question. For EDS, total-count normalization is usually appropriate. For EELS, you may want to normalize to a specific edge. For XRD, peak-height normalization preserves the relative intensities that encode crystal structure.

34. Multi-Spectral and Hyperspectral Data

The Data Cube: \((x, y, E)\)

  • In spectrum imaging, every pixel \((x, y)\) has an associated spectrum \(\mathbf{s}(E)\)
  • The result is a 3D data cube: two spatial dimensions + one spectral dimension
  • Typical sizes:
    • STEM-EDS: \(256 \times 256\) pixels \(\times\) \(2048\) channels \(= 134\) million values
    • STEM-EELS: \(100 \times 100\) pixels \(\times\) \(1024\) channels \(= 10\) million values
  • Naive approach: Unfold to a \(N_{\text{pixels}} \times D_{\text{channels}}\) matrix, apply PCA/AE per spectrum
  • Better approach: Exploit spatial correlations — neighboring pixels likely have similar spectra

Spectrum imaging produces a three-dimensional data cube: two spatial dimensions and one energy dimension. This is exactly analogous to a hyperspectral image in remote sensing. A typical STEM-EDS dataset with 256 by 256 pixels and 2048 energy channels contains 134 million values — about 500 megabytes of raw data for a single measurement. The simplest approach is to treat each pixel’s spectrum independently and apply PCA or an autoencoder to the collection of spectra. This works well and is the most common approach. But it ignores the spatial correlations: neighboring pixels in a homogeneous region should have nearly identical spectra. Exploiting this spatial information can improve both denoising and phase identification, as we will see with spatial-spectral autoencoders.

35. Spatial-Spectral Autoencoders

  • Idea: Use spatial context to improve spectral analysis
  • Architecture: Instead of encoding a single spectrum \(\mathbf{x}(x,y)\), encode a patch of spectra around each pixel
    • Input: \(P \times P \times D\) (e.g., \(5 \times 5 \times 2048\))
    • Use 3D convolutions or factored 2D spatial + 1D spectral convolutions
  • Benefits:
    • Neighboring pixels provide implicit averaging (denoising)
    • Spatial context helps distinguish interface regions from bulk phases
    • Can learn spatially correlated features (e.g., core-shell structures)
  • Trade-off: Spatial smoothing can blur sharp interfaces — choose patch size carefully

Spatial-spectral autoencoders take advantage of the fact that spectrum imaging data has spatial structure. Instead of encoding each spectrum independently, they encode a small spatial patch — say 5 by 5 pixels — and use the spectral information from all 25 pixels in the patch to produce the latent representation for the central pixel. This provides implicit spatial averaging, which is a powerful denoising mechanism: the noise in each pixel is independent, but the signal is correlated across the patch. The architecture typically uses 3D convolutions or factored convolutions that process spatial and spectral dimensions separately. The main trade-off is that spatial smoothing can blur sharp interfaces between phases, so the patch size must be chosen carefully based on the spatial resolution you need.

36. Learning the Peak Dictionary: End-Members

  • Goal: Decompose every spectrum as a non-negative mixture of “pure” end-member spectra

\[\mathbf{x}_i \approx \sum_{k=1}^{K} a_{ik} \, \mathbf{e}_k, \quad a_{ik} \geq 0, \quad \mathbf{e}_k \geq 0\]

  • This is Non-negative Matrix Factorization (NMF) — physically more meaningful than PCA
  • End-members \(\mathbf{e}_k\) resemble actual spectra of pure phases
  • Autoencoder variant: The decoder weights, if constrained to be non-negative, act as a learned dictionary of end-member spectra
  • Advantage over database matching: Learns end-members from the data — accounts for instrument-specific peak shapes and backgrounds

End-member decomposition is the spectral equivalent of unmixing colors. Each spectrum is modeled as a non-negative mixture of pure end-member spectra, where the mixing coefficients represent the fraction of each phase present in the pixel. This is physically motivated: a pixel that is half phase A and half phase B will produce a spectrum that is approximately the average of the two pure spectra. NMF solves this decomposition problem under non-negativity constraints, which ensures that both the end-member spectra and the mixing coefficients are physically meaningful. An autoencoder can be modified to perform a similar decomposition by constraining the decoder weights to be non-negative — the decoder then learns a dictionary of end-member spectra, and the latent codes become the mixing coefficients.

37. Signal Separation: Unmixing Overlapping Peaks

  • Problem: At a grain boundary between phase A and phase B, the measured spectrum is a mixture
  • Linear unmixing: \(\mathbf{x} = \alpha \, \mathbf{e}_A + (1-\alpha) \, \mathbf{e}_B + \boldsymbol{\eta}\)
    • Estimate \(\alpha\) from the latent space position
  • Non-linear unmixing: When mixing is not additive
    • Multiple scattering in EELS
    • Absorption effects in thick EDS specimens
    • Autoencoders handle this naturally through non-linear decoder
  • Application: Sub-pixel composition mapping
    • Even when the beam probe is larger than the feature, unmixing recovers fractional compositions

Signal unmixing is one of the most practically important applications of spectral ML. At interfaces, grain boundaries, and mixed-phase regions, the measured spectrum is a mixture of contributions from multiple phases. If the mixing is linear — which is a good approximation for thin EDS specimens — the problem reduces to estimating the mixing fractions given the end-member spectra. The autoencoder latent space naturally encodes this information: if the AE has learned the end-member spectra, the position of a mixed pixel in latent space tells you the mixing fractions. For non-linear mixing — which occurs in thick specimens or when multiple scattering is important — the non-linear decoder of an autoencoder can model the mixing process more accurately than linear methods.

38. Transfer Learning for Signals: Synthetic to Real

  • Problem: Real spectral datasets for training may be scarce or lack ground truth labels
  • Solution: Pre-train on simulated spectra, fine-tune on real data
  • Simulation tools:
    • XRD: CrystalDiffract, VESTA, Rietveld simulation
    • EELS: FEFF, DFT-based calculations
    • EDS: Monte Carlo (CASINO, DTSA-II)
  • Pipeline:
    1. Simulate spectra for all expected phases with realistic parameters
    2. Add synthetic noise (Poisson + readout noise)
    3. Pre-train DAE or classifier on simulated data
    4. Fine-tune on a small number of real experimental spectra
  • Key: The simulation must capture the relevant physics — peak shapes, backgrounds, artifacts

Transfer learning from simulated to experimental spectra is a powerful strategy for materials science, where labeled experimental data is often scarce. The idea is to use physics-based simulation codes to generate large training datasets with known ground truth. For XRD, you can simulate diffraction patterns for any crystal structure. For EELS, codes like FEFF can calculate edge fine structures from first principles. For EDS, Monte Carlo simulations model the electron-sample interaction and predict the resulting X-ray spectrum. You then add realistic noise and detector effects to the simulated spectra and pre-train your model. Finally, you fine-tune on a small amount of real experimental data to bridge the sim-to-real gap — accounting for instrumental broadening, calibration offsets, and other real-world effects that the simulation might not capture perfectly.

39. Robustness to Instrumental Shifts

  • Real instruments are not perfectly stable:
    • Energy calibration drift (peak positions shift over time)
    • Gain changes (detector sensitivity varies)
    • Contamination buildup (affects background)
  • A model trained on Monday’s data may fail on Friday’s data
  • Strategies for robustness:
  1. Data augmentation: During training, randomly shift, scale, and broaden spectra
  1. Calibration layer: Learn an alignment transformation as part of the model
  1. Domain adaptation: Align feature distributions between source and target instruments
  1. Convolutional architectures: Inherently more robust to small shifts (shift equivariance)

Robustness to instrumental variations is a critical practical concern. Instruments drift: the energy calibration changes with temperature, detector gain varies with age, and contamination builds up on the specimen or the detector window. A model that works perfectly with today’s calibration may fail tomorrow. This is the domain shift problem applied to spectral data. The most practical solution is data augmentation during training: randomly shift peak positions by a few channels, scale intensities, and broaden peaks to simulate the range of instrumental conditions the model might encounter. Convolutional architectures help because they are naturally shift-equivariant — a peak shifted by two channels still activates the same convolutional filter. For more severe shifts, domain adaptation techniques can align the feature distributions from different instruments or different days.

40. Summary: Signal Processing Pipeline

Stage Method Purpose
Preprocessing Normalization, alignment Remove instrumental effects
Reduction PCA or AE Compress to latent representation
Denoising Truncated PCA or DAE Remove noise while preserving signal
Decomposition NMF or constrained AE Identify end-member spectra
Clustering K-means, UMAP in latent space Map spatial phase distribution
Anomaly detection Reconstruction error Flag unexpected features

41. Non-Negative Matrix Factorization (NMF)

The Natural Choice for Spectra

Constraint: Spectra \(\mathbf{X}\) and their components must be non-negative:

\[\mathbf{X} \approx \mathbf{W}\mathbf{H} \quad \text{s.t. } W_{ik}, H_{kj} \ge 0\]

Matrix Characterization Meaning
\(\mathbf{X}\) (\(N \times D\)) Raw dataset (spatial pixels \(\times\) energy channels)
\(\mathbf{W}\) (\(N \times K\)) Abundance maps (spatial distribution of each phase)
\(\mathbf{H}\) (\(K \times D\)) End-member spectra (pure phase signatures)

Why NMF beats PCA for spectra:

  • Interpretability: Components (H) are “real” spectra, not abstract “variations”
  • Physicality: No negative concentrations or negative photon counts
  • Parts-based: Naturally learns additive “building blocks” of the signal

Non-Negative Matrix Factorization is perhaps the most physically intuitive dimensionality reduction method for materials characterization. Unlike PCA, which can have negative eigenvalues and negative loadings — which have no physical meaning for photon counts or chemical concentrations — NMF enforces non-negativity on both the components and the weights. This makes the results immediately interpretable: the H matrix contains the “pure” end-member spectra of the phases present in your sample, and the W matrix contains the abundance maps — the spatial distribution of each phase. NMF naturally learns a “parts-based” representation, identifying the additive building blocks of the signal. This is exactly how we think about multi-phase materials: the measured signal at any pixel is a non-negative sum of non-negative contributions from the individual phases.

Key takeaways:

  • The latent space is the universal interface between raw spectra and scientific insight
  • Choose the right normalization for your data type
  • Always validate against known references and physical expectations

Let me summarize the practical signal processing pipeline. It starts with preprocessing — normalization and calibration — to remove instrumental effects. Then dimensionality reduction via PCA or autoencoders extracts the latent representation. From there, you can denoise, decompose into end-members, cluster into phases, or detect anomalies. The latent space is the central hub: it is where the raw spectral data gets converted into scientifically meaningful information. The choice of normalization, the architecture of the autoencoder, and the clustering method all matter — but the most important thing is to validate your results against known references and physical expectations. ML is a tool, not a black box: you must always ask whether the results make physical sense.

Section 5: Case Studies

From XRD Phase ID to Real-time Signal Monitoring

In this final section, we look at specific case studies that demonstrate the techniques from this unit in action. These examples span different characterization techniques and different materials systems, showing the breadth of applications for ML-based spectral analysis.

42. Case Study: Automatic XRD Phase Identification

Problem

  • Given a noisy XRD pattern, identify which crystallographic phases are present
  • Traditional: Match peaks against the ICDD database (manual, slow, error-prone for mixtures)

ML Approach

  1. Train an autoencoder on simulated XRD patterns for all candidate phases
  2. Encode the unknown pattern into latent space
  3. Use nearest-neighbor classification or clustering in latent space

Results

  • Handles multi-phase mixtures by decomposition in latent space
  • Robust to preferred orientation and peak broadening (which confuse database matching)
  • Can detect amorphous phases as anomalies (high reconstruction error)

XRD phase identification is one of the most common tasks in materials characterization. The traditional approach is to match peak positions and intensities against a database like the ICDD Powder Diffraction File. This works well for single-phase samples with good crystallinity, but struggles with multi-phase mixtures, preferred orientation, peak broadening, and amorphous phases. An autoencoder trained on simulated patterns for all candidate phases learns a latent representation where different phases cluster in different regions. An unknown pattern is encoded into this space, and its phase assignment is determined by which cluster it falls into. For multi-phase samples, the latent coordinates can be decomposed into contributions from each phase. This approach is faster and more robust than manual database matching, especially for high-throughput applications.

43. Case Study: EELS Spectrum Imaging — Fe\(^{2+}\)/Fe\(^{3+}\) Mapping

Problem

  • Map the spatial distribution of iron oxidation states at nanometer resolution
  • The Fe-L\(_{2,3}\) white line ratio (\(L_3/L_2\)) and onset energy differ between Fe\(^{2+}\) and Fe\(^{3+}\)

ML Approach

  • Train a denoising autoencoder on simulated Fe-L edges for both oxidation states
  • Apply to experimental EELS spectrum image pixel by pixel
  • Use the latent space to map oxidation state continuously (not just binary classification)

Impact

  • Enables oxidation state mapping at 5x lower dose than conventional analysis
  • Captures continuous gradients (e.g., mixed-valence regions at interfaces)
  • Validated against reference spectra from known Fe\(^{2+}\) and Fe\(^{3+}\) standards

This is a showcase application for denoising autoencoders in electron microscopy. Iron oxides are ubiquitous in materials science and geoscience, and the oxidation state of iron — whether it is two-plus or three-plus — has major implications for magnetic properties, catalytic activity, and corrosion behavior. The traditional method for determining oxidation state from EELS is to measure the L3 to L2 white line ratio, but this requires high signal-to-noise spectra, which means high electron dose. A denoising autoencoder trained on simulated spectra can recover the fine structure from noisy low-dose data, enabling oxidation state mapping with 5x less dose. The continuous latent representation also captures mixed-valence regions, which a binary classification would miss.

44. Case Study: Large-Scale EDS Maps

The Scale Challenge

  • Modern SEM/STEM-EDS: \(512 \times 512\) pixels, 2048 channels = ~500,000 spectra
  • Multiple fields of view → millions of spectra per sample

PCA-Based Pipeline

  1. Apply PCA to the unfolded data cube (\(N \times D\) matrix)
  2. Denoise: Reconstruct using top-\(K\) components (typically \(K = 10\text{--}20\))
  3. Cluster in score space using K-means
  4. Map cluster assignments back to spatial coordinates → phase map

Results

  • Denoised maps reveal trace elements below the raw noise floor
  • Phase maps identify 5-8 distinct phases automatically
  • Processing time: Minutes on a standard workstation (PCA is fast!)

Large-scale EDS mapping is one of the most common applications of PCA in materials science. A single high-resolution EDS map can contain half a million spectra, and analyzing an entire sample may require tiling many such maps. PCA is the ideal tool here because it is fast — SVD of the data matrix takes seconds to minutes — and scales linearly with the number of spectra. The denoising capability is particularly valuable: by reconstructing from the top components, you can detect trace elements that are invisible in the raw data because their peaks are below the noise floor. Clustering the PCA scores then produces an automatic phase map. This pipeline — PCA denoising followed by K-means clustering — is the standard in many electron microscopy labs and has been implemented in commercial software packages.

45. Clustering in Latent Space: UMAP Visualization

From Latent Codes to Phase Maps (Sandfeld et al. 2024)

  • After dimensionality reduction (PCA or AE), each spectrum is a point \(\mathbf{z}_i \in \mathbb{R}^K\)
  • UMAP (Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection) projects \(K\)-dimensional latent codes to 2D for visualization
    • Preserves both local and global structure (better than t-SNE for this)

  • Clusters in UMAP space correspond to distinct material phases
  • Bridges between clusters indicate transition regions (interfaces, diffusion zones)
  • Outliers flag anomalies or rare phases

UMAP visualization of the latent space is an extremely powerful way to understand your spectral dataset. After encoding all spectra into the latent space — whether using PCA scores or autoencoder latent codes — you apply UMAP to project these K-dimensional vectors into two dimensions for plotting. Well-separated clusters correspond to distinct material phases. The spatial extent of each cluster tells you about the compositional variability within each phase. Bridges between clusters indicate interface regions where spectra are mixtures of two phases. Isolated outlier points flag anomalies — unexpected phases, contamination, or instrument artifacts. This is discussed in Sandfeld Section 15.6. The combination of an autoencoder for latent encoding plus UMAP for visualization provides a complete exploratory analysis framework for spectral data.

46. Discovering New Phases with Anomaly Detection

When the Model Says “I Don’t Know”

  • Train an autoencoder on spectra from known phases
  • Apply it to a new sample — most spectra reconstruct well
  • High reconstruction error pixels indicate spectra that differ from all known phases
  • Discovery workflow:
    1. Map reconstruction error spatially → anomaly map
    2. Extract spectra from high-error regions
    3. Inspect these spectra manually → identify the unknown phase
    4. Add to the training set and retrain
  • Example: Discovery of an unexpected intermetallic compound at a diffusion couple interface
    • The AE was trained on the two base metals
    • High reconstruction error at the interface revealed a third phase

Anomaly detection using autoencoders can lead to genuine scientific discoveries. The workflow is straightforward: train the autoencoder on spectra from known or expected phases, then apply it to new data. Most pixels will reconstruct well, but some will have unexpectedly high reconstruction errors. These are the anomalies — spectra that do not fit any of the patterns the autoencoder has learned. By mapping the reconstruction error spatially, you can see exactly where the anomalous regions are. Extract those spectra, inspect them, and you may discover a new phase, an unexpected reaction product, or contamination. This has been demonstrated in diffusion couple studies, where an autoencoder trained on the two base metals flagged a narrow intermetallic layer at the interface that had not been anticipated.

47. Real-time Signal Monitoring

Closing the Loop: ML During Acquisition

  • Conventional workflow: Acquire → Store → Analyze offline
  • ML-enabled workflow: Acquire → Analyze in real time → Adjust acquisition
  • Applications:
    • Monitor reconstruction error during scanning — stop early when all phases are mapped
    • Detect beam damage by tracking spectral changes in real time
    • Adaptive acquisition: Spend more time on interesting regions, less on uniform areas
  • Requirements:
    • Model must be fast enough for real-time inference (PCA: trivially fast; small AE: ~ms per spectrum)
    • Must handle streaming data (incremental PCA, online AE updates)

The Future Is Adaptive

Combining real-time ML with instrument control creates self-driving microscopes that autonomously explore materials, guided by the scientific questions encoded in the ML model. This is the topic of Unit 11.

Real-time signal monitoring is the frontier of ML-enabled characterization. Instead of collecting all the data and analyzing it offline, you can run the ML model during acquisition. A PCA projection takes microseconds per spectrum; a small autoencoder takes milliseconds on a GPU. This enables real-time phase mapping, real-time anomaly detection, and adaptive acquisition strategies where the instrument spends more time on scientifically interesting regions and less time on uniform background. You can also monitor for beam damage by tracking how the latent representation of a pixel changes over successive scans. This concept of closing the loop between the instrument and the ML model is the foundation for self-driving microscopy, which we will explore in Unit 11.

48. Exercise Preview

Hands-on: PCA and Autoencoder for EDS Spectra

Part 1: PCA Analysis

  1. Load a simulated EDS spectrum image (100 \(\times\) 100 pixels, 1024 channels, 3 phases)
  2. Compute PCA and plot the scree plot — determine the intrinsic dimensionality
  3. Reconstruct the data using \(K = 3, 5, 10\) components — compare denoised spectra
  4. Plot PCA score maps and identify the three phases

Part 2: Autoencoder

  1. Build a fully-connected autoencoder with bottleneck size 4
  2. Train on the same dataset — compare reconstruction loss vs. PCA
  3. Visualize the latent space and compare clustering to PCA scores
  4. Introduce an “unknown” phase in a few pixels — use reconstruction error to detect it

Part 3: Denoising

  1. Add Poisson noise at various dose levels — compare PCA and DAE denoising

The exercise for this unit will give you hands-on experience with both PCA and autoencoders for spectral data. You will work with a simulated EDS dataset — three phases with realistic peak shapes and noise levels — so you can compare your results against the ground truth. The exercise progresses from basic PCA analysis through autoencoder training to a practical comparison of denoising methods. The anomaly detection task in Part 2 is particularly instructive: you will see how an autoencoder trained on three phases flags pixels from an unexpected fourth phase. This exercise will be available as a Jupyter notebook.

49. Summary and Key Takeaways

What We Learned Today

  1. Characterization signals (XRD, EELS, EDS, XPS, Raman) are high-dimensional but lie on low-dimensional manifolds
  1. PCA provides fast, linear dimensionality reduction — excellent for denoising and compression, but limited to linear variations
  1. Autoencoders extend PCA to non-linear settings — handling peak shifts, shape changes, and complex backgrounds
  1. Denoising autoencoders explicitly learn to map noisy spectra to clean ones — enabling lower-dose characterization
  1. The latent space is the universal interface: it enables compression, denoising, clustering, anomaly detection, and visualization
  1. Practical pipelines require careful normalization, validation, and robustness to instrumental shifts

Next unit: Unit 11 — Automation in Microscopy (closing the loop)

Let me summarize the key takeaways from this unit. Characterization signals are high-dimensional vectors that lie on low-dimensional manifolds determined by the physics of the material. PCA is the right first tool — fast, deterministic, and effective for linear variations. Autoencoders generalize PCA to non-linear settings and offer additional capabilities through variants like denoising and variational autoencoders. The latent space — whether from PCA or an autoencoder — is the central concept that connects all downstream analysis tasks. In practice, careful preprocessing, validation, and robustness engineering are essential. Next week, in Unit 11, we will see how these spectral analysis tools can be deployed in real time to create self-driving microscopes that autonomously explore materials.

50. References

Core References

  • Sandfeld et al. (2024): Ch. 15 (PCA), Ch. 15.3.2 (Scree Plots), Ch. 15.3.3 (Denoising), Ch. 15.6 (Clustering), Ch. 19.4 (Autoencoders)
  • McClarren (2021): Ch. 8.3.2 (Denoising Autoencoders)
  • Neuer et al. (2024): Ch. 5 (Unsupervised Learning)

Further Reading

  • Kingma & Welling (2014): “Auto-Encoding Variational Bayes” — the original VAE paper
  • Lee & Seung (1999): “Learning the parts of objects by non-negative matrix factorization” — NMF
  • McInnes et al. (2018): “UMAP: Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection” — dimensionality reduction for visualization

Software

  • HyperSpy: Open-source Python library for multi-dimensional spectral data analysis
  • scikit-learn: PCA, NMF, K-means implementations
  • PyTorch / TensorFlow: Autoencoder implementations
McClarren, Ryan G. 2021. Machine Learning for Engineers: Using Data to Solve Problems for Physical Systems. Springer.
Neuer, Michael et al. 2024. Machine Learning for Engineers: Introduction to Physics-Informed, Explainable Learning Methods for AI in Engineering Applications. Springer Nature.
Sandfeld, Stefan et al. 2024. Materials Data Science. Springer.

Here are the key references for this unit. The Sandfeld textbook covers PCA, scree plots, denoising, and autoencoders in detail. McClarren covers denoising autoencoders. For practical implementation, HyperSpy is the standard Python library for multi-dimensional spectral data in electron microscopy, and it includes built-in PCA and NMF routines. For autoencoders, you would use PyTorch or TensorFlow. The UMAP paper by McInnes is an excellent read if you want to understand the mathematics behind the visualization method. I encourage you to explore these references as you work through the exercise.

© Philipp Pelz - Machine Learning in Materials Processing & Characterization
